scope: Accessibility standards used by the reusable UI and UX engineer agent templates.

claims.claim: WCAG 2.2 is a W3C Recommendation with testable A, AA, and AAA success criteria, and W3C recommends adopting WCAG 2.2 as the current conformance target.

project_evidence.value: The UI and UX engineer roles must account for accessibility standards.

target.configuration: Level AA default engineering target unless project requirements specify another target

source.title: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2
source.authority_evidence: The normative specification is published by W3C on w3.org.
source.target_match_evidence: The source is the versioned WCAG 2.2 Recommendation selected for these role templates.
source.freshness_rationale: The role pins the versioned WCAG 2.2 Recommendation rather than an unversioned summary.

claims.evidence: WCAG 2.2 defines testable success criteria and conformance levels, and its introduction recommends adoption of WCAG 2.2 as the new conformance target.
claims.decision: Use WCAG 2.2 Level AA as the default engineering target while requiring project-specific obligations to override it and prohibiting unsupported conformance claims.

claims.claim: The WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide provides patterns for semantics and keyboard interaction but is informative guidance, not a normative accessibility standard or UI design system.

project_evidence.value: The UI and UX roles need implementation guidance for accessible custom widgets without conflating guidance with conformance.

target.configuration: Accessible web patterns, semantics, and keyboard interaction

source.title: ARIA Authoring Practices Guide: Introduction
source.section: Purpose of the APG; APG is Not a Normative Standard; APG is not a UI Design System
source.authority_evidence: The guide is published by the W3C Web Accessibility Initiative on w3.org.
source.target_match_evidence: The source describes the current APG used for accessible semantics, widget patterns, and keyboard guidance.
source.freshness_rationale: APG is maintained as living guidance, so it receives the policy maximum 120-day review window.

claims.evidence: APG synthesizes accessible web pattern guidance and keyboard conventions while stating that it is not a normative standard or UI design system.
claims.decision: Use APG to inform custom-widget behavior only after preferring native semantics; never cite APG use as proof of WCAG conformance.
